1: nebukhad-netstsar malka lekhol-amaya umevaya ulishanaya (nĕḇûḵad-neṣṣar malkāʾ lĕḵol-ʿammayyāʾ ûmĕʾayyāʾ wĕliššānayyāʾ): Nebuchadnezzar the king to all peoples, nations, and languages. Chapter 4 is unique in Scripture: a Gentile king's first-person testimony and praise of YHWH, framed as a royal letter to the whole empire. The Aramaic word shelamkhon yisgeh (may your peace increase) echoes the standard greeting of royal Aramaic letters (identical to the Elephantine papyri and Ezra's correspondence). The literary form authenticates the setting.
2: atayya v'timhaya (ʾāṯayyāʾ wĕṯimhāyyāʾ): signs and wonders. The Aramaic pair is the standard formula for divine acts of power — used consistently in Daniel (Daniel 6:27-28) and paralleled in Acts 2:22 for Christ's mighty works. Shaphir qadamai (it is a good thing before me): the king judges that his testimony is worthy — a self-aware editorial note from the narrator (Nebuchadnezzar speaking as if from a position of restored sanity and faith).
3: malkhuteh malkhut alam (malkûṯêh malkûṯ ʿālam): his kingdom is an everlasting kingdom. The Aramaic alam (world/eternity) is cognate to the Hebrew olam. This confession in Daniel 4:3 is repeated almost verbatim in Daniel 4:34 after Nebuchadnezzar's humiliation — it frames the chapter: the king knows the truth before his madness and confesses it again after. The phrase recurs at 7:14 for the Son of Man's dominion — the eternal kingdom language reaches its Christological fulfillment there.
4: shale (šĕlēh): at ease, prospering, tranquil. The Aramaic root shelah (peace/security) describes Nebuchadnezzar's state before the vision. Raʿnan (flourishing/luxuriant) is a word normally applied to trees and plants — used here metaphorically of the king's prosperity, foreshadowing the tree-image of Daniel 4:10-12. The dream comes in this moment of peak prosperity: the highest point of human achievement becomes the site of divine intervention.
5: khezve de-yalbunani (ḥĕzwê dî-yḇalʿunannî): dreams that terrified me. The Aramaic root bela (swallow/terrify) is vivid — the dreams consumed him. Dimyonaya al-mishkevi (images upon my bed) uses the plural dimyona (imaginings/visions) for the frightening content. The juxtaposition of prosperity (Daniel 4:4) and terror (Daniel 4:5) is the narrative hinge: success does not protect from divine warning.
6: belteshatzar rav kharshaya (bēlṭĕšaṣṣar rab ḥarṭummayāʾ): Belteshazzar, chief of the magicians. Daniel's Babylonian court name (belteshatzar = “Bel protect his life” or “may Bel protect the king”) is used by the narrator here in the king's speech — but the Aramaic adds di-ruach elahim qadishim beh (in whom is the spirit of the holy gods), the same formula the queen uses in Daniel 5:11. The court name conceals a Hebrew reality; the spirit-formula reveals it.
7: v'acharon ul Daniel (wĕʾaḥărîn ʿāl Dāniyyēʾl): and finally Daniel came in. The detail that Daniel comes in last — after all the other wise men have failed — heightens his presentation as the one who alone has the spirit. The narrative echoes Daniel 2: the wise men fail, Daniel succeeds. Pesha (interpretation) is the Aramaic technical term for dream-interpretation used throughout Daniel 2 and 4-5.
8: di-ruach elahim qadishim beh (dî-rûaḥ ʾĕlāhîn qaddîšîn bēhî): in whom is the spirit of the holy gods. The plural elahim qadishim (holy gods) again reflects the king's polytheistic frame, but his consistent use of this formula for Daniel suggests he has identified Daniel with a unique divine source. The same phrase appears in Daniel 5:11, 14 — it is the queen's formula and then the king's. The spirit that distinguishes Daniel from all others is YHWH's spirit, misidentified by the Babylonian frame as the spirit of “holy gods” (plural).
9: rav kharshaya (rab ḥarṭummayāʾ): master of the magicians. The Aramaic chartom (magician/diviner) is possibly an Egyptian loanword (ḥr-tp or similar) carried into the ANE court tradition. Nebuchadnezzar addresses Daniel as the superior of the court wisdom class — but then appeals to the spirit that transcends that class. The address la ya'aqer manach raz (no secret troubles you) establishes Daniel's competence before the request.
10: ilana begoah shemayya (ʾîlānāʾ begoah šemayyāʾ): the tree in the middle of the earth / at the height of heaven. The dream-vision opens with the cosmic tree motif familiar from Mesopotamian iconography: a world-tree whose branches reach heaven, whose roots go deep. The Aramaic ilana (tree) appears seven times in Daniel 4:10-23. The cosmic tree is a standard image for empire in ANE literature — cf. Ezekiel 31:3-9 (Assyria as cedars of Lebanon). The height reaching heaven (rugbeh limshet lishemayya) echoes the tower of Babel (ve-rosho vashamayim, Genesis 11:4).
11: sgav ilana v'taqef (sgā ʾîlānāʾ wetqēp̄): the tree grew tall and became strong. The two verbs — sga (to grow large/magnify) and tqep (to become strong) — describe a progressive intensification. Ad-shamayya (to the heavens): Aramaic shamayya (the heavens) is the same word in Aramaic and Hebrew. The visibility clause — “it was visible to the end of all the earth” — is imperial hyperbole appropriate for a world-empire: what reaches to heaven is visible everywhere.
12: The tree providing food and shelter for all creatures: tepeh saggi (abundant fruit), meta lesavah lekhola (food for all). The tree as provider of food, shade, and shelter echoes the parable of the mustard seed (Matthew 13:32) — a tree under which the birds of the air nest. The Babylonian tree-image (power as shelter) becomes in the NT the Christ-image (kingdom as shelter). The Aramaic detail that “all flesh was fed from it” (kol-beshar yitnezuun minneh) is universal — the tree feeds every living thing.
13: ir v'qaddish (ʿîr wĕqaddîš): a watcher and a holy one. The ir (watcher/waking one) is an angelic being unique to Daniel in the canonical OT (though appearing in the Enoch literature). The term iqad (watcher/one who watches) suggests a heavenly being who observes and enforces divine decisions. Paired with qaddish (holy one), it is a doublet for a single angelic figure. The heavenly court language that dominates Daniel 4-5 and 7 positions YHWH as the true king whose heavenly council issues binding decrees over human kingdoms.
14: gadu ilana (gûdû ʾîlānāʾ): cut down the tree. The imperative gadu is from the root geda (to cut down) — the same verb used in the OT for felling trees (Deuteronomy 19:5). The watcher's decree is juridical: badar periage (scatter its fruit) — the reversal of the tree's provision. The stump is left with a band of iron and bronze: ikar shorshohi b'ara (leave its roots/stump in the earth) — a crucial detail that signals the judgment is not final elimination but temporary humiliation with restoration in view.
15: ikar shorshohi (ʾiqqar šorĕšôhî): the stump of its roots. Ikar is the tree-stump — what remains after felling. The binding of the stump with iron and bronze (be-essar di-parzel unechash) prevents regrowth during the seven times. Then the pivotal pronoun shift: “let his heart be changed from a man's, and let an animal's heart be given to him” — the tree suddenly becomes the king. The Aramaic levav anasha (heart of a man) → levav chaiva (heart of an animal) describes the loss of rational dignity (tselem elahim, the image of God in human terms).
16: levav enasha yishnon mineh (libbēhî min-ʾĕnāšāʾ yĕšannaʾ): let his heart be changed from a man's. The verb shanna (to change/alter) was used in 3:28 for the angel who changed the king's fire-decree. Now it describes the reversal of rational faculty. levav chaiva yityhav leh (an animal's heart be given to him): the passive form (yityhav = let it be given) uses the divine passive — God is the implied agent. The number shiv'a idanin (seven times/seasons) uses the ambiguous idan — period, season, time — which may mean seven years or seven seasons.
17: bigzerat iryin petagma uvammar qadishin shuʾleta (bigĕzêrat ʿîrîn pittĕgāmāʾ ûbimēʾmar qaddîšîn šūʾaltāʾ): by the decree of the watchers the sentence and by the word of the holy ones the decision. The juridical language of the heavenly court is precise: gezera (decree/sentence), petagam (word/decision), shuʾalta (request/demand). The heavenly council issues binding decisions about earthly kingdoms. The stated purpose: di-ydaʿ chayya di-shalit ʿilaya b'malkhut anasha (that the living may know that the Most High rules in the kingdom of men) — the theological thesis of Daniel's entire vision of history.
18: The king asks Daniel to interpret because kol-khakimei malkhuti la yakhlin hodaʿutani peshara (all the wise men of my kingdom could not make known to me the interpretation) — the court wisdom system has failed again (cf. Daniel 2). The Aramaic hkhim (wise) is the comprehensive term for the Babylonian learned class. Daniel's superiority is framed in terms of the spirit: ruach elahim qadishim (spirit of holy gods), not superior training or skill.
19: Belteshatzar etdahar sha'ah akhat (bēlṭĕšaṣṣar etdahar šāʿah ḥăḏāh): Belteshazzar was alarmed for a moment. Etdahar (was alarmed/horrified) uses the same root as 4:5 for the king's terror. Sha'ah akhat (one hour/moment) — a brief pause of horror at the vision's meaning. Then the king explicitly invites Daniel's honest report: khezva ve-peshara la yevahalanek (let not the dream or its interpretation trouble you) — the king, for once, wants the truth even if it is about himself.
20: Daniel's description of the tree restates Daniel 4:10-12 almost verbatim — the repetition is formal, legally confirming the interpretation against the original vision. The Aramaic narrative technique of repetition-for-confirmation appears throughout Daniel: visions are restated (Daniel 2:31-35 restated in Daniel 2:38-44), decrees are restated (Daniel 3:4-6 restated in Daniel 3:10-11). The mirroring of original statement and interpretation signals truthfulness and completeness.
21: The tree whose branches housed the birds, whose shade sheltered the animals, from whose fruit all flesh was fed: these details are restated in the interpretation to prepare for the application. Kol-beshar mineh yitnezuun (all flesh was fed from it): the universality of Nebuchadnezzar's empire-as-provision is the ground of the judgment — he used what YHWH gave him as if he had made it himself (cf. Ezekiel 29:3: “the Nile is mine and I made it”).
22: ant-hu malka (ʾantāh-hûʾ malkāʾ): it is you, O king. The application is direct and personal. The Aramaic pronoun ant-hu (you-he) is emphatic. The phrase di-revat v'etqefat (who grew great and became strong) mirrors the tree's description exactly — the king is the tree. Ve-riavat rugbak ad-shamayya (and your greatness has reached heaven): the same tower-of-Babel language that described the tree now describes Nebuchadnezzar — the echo is unmistakable.
23: The watcher's decree restated in summary: cut down the tree, but leave the stump. The connecting purpose (di-shalit ʿIllaya b'malkhut anasha: that the Most High rules in the kingdom of men) appears again — this is the sentence's stated rationale. The threefold repetition of this sentence (Daniel 4:17, 25, 32) in Daniel 4 is Daniel's theological drumbeat: all human sovereignty is derivative, conditional, and reversible.
24: dena peshara malka (dĕnāh pišrāʾ malkāʾ): this is the interpretation, O king. The formal announcement of the interpretation. Pigra Ilai et-ot malka rabayta (the decree of the Most High has reached my lord the king): the Aramaic mata (reached) suggests the decree has arrived like a legal document. The king stands under a sentence that has already been issued from the heavenly court — the only question is when it will be executed.
25: v'lak letardoon min anasha (wĕlāk yṭardûn min-ʾĕnāšāʾ): you will be driven from men. The passive yitardoon (will be driven) uses the divine passive — the same God who issued the decree in the heavenly court will execute it. Im-chayat bara tekhul (you will eat grass like an ox): the animal humiliation described. shiv'a idanin (seven times) restated: the duration is fixed. The purpose: di-teda di-shalit ʿIllaya (that you may know that the Most High rules) — the personal, second-person application of the theological thesis.
26: v'di amaru lishbaq ikar shorshohi di ilana (wĕdî ʾămarû lišbaq ʿiqqar šorĕšôhî dî-ʾîlānāʾ): and as for the command to leave the stump of the tree's roots. The stump-preservation is interpreted as the guarantee of Nebuchadnezzar's restoration: malkhutakh lekhak tiqum (your kingdom will be confirmed for you) after you know that Heaven rules. The Aramaic shamayya shalit (Heaven rules) substitutes Heaven as a circumlocution for YHWH — a reverent substitution also found in the phrase malkut shamayya (kingdom of Heaven) in the NT (Matthew 3:2; Daniel 4:17).
27: la-ken malka mashlakhani yishtaveh (lāhēn malkāʾ mšaḥlĕḥānnî yiqqabal): therefore, O king, let my counsel be acceptable to you. Daniel's exhortation to repentance: peruk khatayak bitzidqah (break off your sins by righteousness) and avatek banot anaya (your iniquities by showing mercy to the poor). The two imperatives (pruk = break off/atone; anay = afflicted/poor) constitute a summary of OT ethics: righteousness and mercy. The conditional hope: hn tihye arokhat shlevtak (perhaps there will be a lengthening of your tranquility) — the judgment may be postponed if repentance occurs.
28: All this came upon Nebuchadnezzar: the narrative resumes after the interpretation with the bare statement of fulfillment. The Aramaic kol-dena mata (all this came) uses the verb meta (to arrive/come) — the decree from the heavenly court has arrived. The time marker leqets yarkheen tri-eshar (at the end of twelve months) shows the delay: one full year elapsed between the dream/interpretation and the judgment. Daniel's exhortation (Daniel 4:27) had been followed — at least temporarily.
29: al heikhal malkhuta di-Bavel mehhalekh havah (ʿal hêḵal malkûṯāʾ dî-bābel mĕhallēk hăwāh): on the palace of the kingdom of Babylon he was walking. The setting of the boast: the roof of the palace, looking over the city. Mihallekh havah (was walking) uses the Aramaic participial construction for ongoing action — he was strolling, luxuriating in the view. The timing — at the end of the twelve months, during a moment of prosperous ease — is the narrative's ironic precision: the judgment falls at the height of complacency.
30: la-da Bavel rabta di-ana benayit (hălāʾ dāʾ bābel rabbĕṯāʾ dî-ʾănāh bĕnayit): Is not this great Babylon that I built? The boast contains three claims: ana benayit (I built), livet malkhuta (for the house of the kingdom), bitqef khutsani (by my mighty power). Historical confirmation: Nebuchadnezzar was the greatest builder-king in Babylonian history; the surviving texts of his building inscriptions use nearly identical language (“I built Babylon, the great city, my royal residence”). The irony is that his own inscriptions confirm the accuracy of the biblical portrait — and their boast is the sin Daniel warned against.
31: od pithgama b'pum malka (ʿôḏ millĕṯāʾ bepum malkāʾ): while the word was still in the king's mouth. The immediacy is the literary point: the divine verdict falls before the sentence is finished. The Aramaic bat qal nefal min-shamayya (a voice fell from heaven) — bat qal (daughter of the voice) is the technical term in rabbinic literature for a divine voice from heaven, lesser than prophecy but still authoritative. The voice declares: malkhuta avadat minnakh (the kingdom has departed from you) — a perfect/completed declaration.
32: The prophetic declaration is immediate and specific: min-anasha tetarad (you will be driven from men), im-chayat bara mevakhak lakh (with the animals of the field your dwelling will be). The threefold repetition of shiv'a idanin (seven times) throughout the chapter has built to this moment of execution. The declarative purpose — di-teda di-shalit ʿIllaya b'malkhut anasha (that you may know that the Most High rules in the kingdom of men) — echoes Daniel 4:17 and 25. The purpose of YHWH's judgment is always revelation.
33: ba-shetaʿa fulfilled upon Nebuchadnezzar (beh-šaʿtāʾ millĕṯāʾ sāpat ʿal nĕḇûḵaḏ-neṣṣar): at that very hour the word was fulfilled. The Aramaic ba-shetaʿa (at that hour/moment) is emphatic immediacy — no delay between sentence and execution. The medical symptoms described (min-anasha tetarad, v'asba chaiva yikhul, u-ketal-shamayya yitsatabah: driven from men, eating grass like cattle, his body bathed in the dew of heaven) may reflect the condition known as boanthropy — a psychiatric syndrome documented in ancient Near Eastern medical texts and confirmed by modern case studies.
34: ulqats yomaya ana nebukhad-netstsar einai lishemayya natlet (ûliqṣāṯ yômayāʾ ʾănāh nĕḇûḵaḏ-neṣṣar ʿênay lišmayyāʾ nĕṭĕlēṯ): and at the end of the days I, Nebuchadnezzar, lifted my eyes to heaven. The raising of eyes to heaven is the recovery of the rational-and-spiritual faculty: umdaʿi yetuv ʿalai (and my reason returned to me). The first act of restored reason is the praise of YHWH: ulilaiay berakhit (and I blessed the Most High). The verb berakh (bless/kneel) signals worship-posture. The confession that follows: malkhuteh malkhut alam v'shaltehneh im-dar v'dar (his kingdom is an everlasting kingdom and his dominion is from generation to generation) — the same formula as Daniel 4:3, now confessed from experience rather than introduced as preface.
35: v'kol-dayrei arʿa k'la khashiveen (wĕḵol-dāyĕrê ʾarʿāʾ k'lāʾ ḥăšîḇîn): all the inhabitants of the earth are accounted as nothing. K'la (as nothing/zero) is absolute: compared to YHWH's sovereignty, all earthly inhabitors are nothing. The phrase v'kitsvehteh ʿaved bishemayya uvimdinat arʿa (and he does his will in the host of heaven and among the inhabitants of earth) uses the verb ʿaved (does/makes) — YHWH's will is executed in both the heavenly and earthly realms simultaneously. The rhetorical question v'la-itai man di-yemacher b'yedehheh (there is no one who can stay his hand) closes with absolute unilateral sovereignty.
36: b'idana dena madaʿi yetub ʿalai (bizdān dĕnāh maddi ʿalai ytûb): at the same time my reason returned to me. The restoration formula is threefold: hadaru heder malkhuti (the majesty of my kingdom was restored), v'labibeday itub lehon (and my reason returned to me), and yattirey revavet (I was greatly increased). The Aramaic hadar (splendor/majesty) is a royal term — the same word used for YHWH's glory in the Psalms (Psalms 29:4; Daniel 96:6). The restoration of human dignity is described in the same terms as divine glory — another echo of the tselem elahim (image of God) principle.
37: kaan ana nebukhad-netstsar meshabbech u-meromem umehaddra le-malik shemayya (kĕʿan ʾănāh nĕḇûḵaḏ-neṣṣar mĕšabbaḥ ûmĕrômem ûmĕhaddêr lĕmelek šemayyāʾ): now I, Nebuchadnezzar, praise and extol and honor the King of Heaven. The triple participle construction (meshabbech, meromem, mehaddra = praising, extolling, honoring) is a liturgical formula — the language of worship applied to YHWH by a Gentile king. The final confession: di-ʿavdeh qeshet (whose works are truth) and urkhev-oteh din (whose ways are justice) — both attributes of YHWH throughout the OT. The closing warning: v'di-mehalkheen begava yakhil le-heshpalah (those who walk in pride he is able to humble) — using the verb yakhil (is able) that the three men used of YHWH's ability to deliver them.
